subject: Statistics
title: Braess's Paradox
description: A quick win: understand Braess's Paradox.

hook.text: A city builds a new road to reduce traffic. Congestion gets worse. Adding capacity made the network slower.

buildup.text: Braess's paradox: adding a shortcut to a network can slow everyone down when each driver acts selfishly.

discovery.text: Each driver takes the individually fastest route. But when everyone does, the shortcut becomes a bottleneck.

twist.text: Closing roads has actually improved traffic in cities. Seoul removed an elevated highway and traffic got better.

climax.text: The paradox occurs in electrical networks, internet routing, and basketball — adding a star player can slow the team.

punchline.text: More roads, more traffic. Individual optimization defeats collective good.

quiz.question: What causes Braess's paradox in traffic networks?
options: Each driver chooses the individually fastest route, but collectively this creates worse congestion
New roads are always built poorly
